careers.notes: Per Wikipedia: 'Willard Hooker had held roles such as vice president and treasurer' in Hooker Electrochemical. Likely refers to Horace Willard. Fourth engineer-brother in this Rochester generation.

notable.notable_blurb: Married his Pierpont-line Hooker cousin Ruth Ward

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Hooker cousin via Pierpont line
narrative_blocks.body: On 31 May 1906 Horace married Ruth Ward, daughter of Rev. George Kemp Ward and Caroline Editha Pierpont. Through her mother Ruth was herself a Hooker descendant via the Pierpont line — making the marriage one of the project's documented cousin marriages, with both spouses tracing descent to Rev. Thomas Hooker through different branches.

narrative_blocks.header: Treasurer of a New York investment firm
narrative_blocks.body: Per Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy, Horace was treasurer of the Development and Funding Co. in New York City. He continued the engineering and finance patterns of his immediate Hooker forebears — his father Horace B. the Civil War engineer, and his older brother Elon Huntington Hooker the Hooker Electrochemical founder.

cross_connections.display_label: Married a Hooker cousin descended through the Pierpont line

career.notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909: treasurer of the Development and Funding Co. of New York City.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Horace Willard Hooker Sr. (H03437) descends from Thomas Hooker via the Horace James Hooker line.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Ruth Ward Hooker (HD3400) descends from Thomas Hooker via her mother Caroline Editha Pierpont (HD1545) and the Pierpont line. As both parents are Hooker descendants, Horace Jr. is a dual-line descendant.
Mother Ruth Ward Hooker (HD3400). Middle name 'Ward' carries his maternal grandfather Rev. George Kemp Ward (X01665) and his mother's maiden line.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his maternal grandfather Rev. George Kemp Ward and his mother's maiden surname.

sources.label: Princeton Magazine, Vol. 6 (1891), pp. 428-9 — biographical sketch

institutions.institution_blurb: Class of 1869, A.M. 1872. Class Secretary and Historian; American Whig Society; Iota Chapter of Delta Psi.

education.notes: Preliminary training; later attended the University of Rochester in his Freshman year before transferring to Princeton.
Princeton class of 1869; member of the American Whig Society; Iota Chapter of Delta Psi (joined at Rochester); elected Secretary and Historian of his class.
Prepared for the ministry of the Presbyterian Church.

career.notes: First and only pastoral charge — twenty-five years. Twice Moderator of the Presbytery of Rochester; editor of the Presbyterian Church Herald; member of the Livingston County Historical Society.

career.organization: Association of Descendants of Andrew Ward
career.notes: Compiler of the Andrew Ward Family Genealogy.

Published two volumes of class history: 'After Nine Years' (1878) and 'The Vigintennial Volume of 1869' (1889) — a finely illustrated twenty-year record of the Princeton class. Also published several religious pamphlets including 'The Strength of Young Manhood' and 'The Memorial of Elizabeth Dippy'.

narrative_blocks.header: Switched colleges mid-freshman year for Princeton
narrative_blocks.body: George began his college career at the University of Rochester but, midway through his Freshman year, transferred to Princeton — bringing his Iota Chapter of Delta Psi membership with him. He graduated as Old Nassau's Class Secretary and Historian in 1869, then earned his A.M. in 1872 and finished at Princeton Theological Seminary the next year.

After ordination George took the pastorate at Dansville Presbyterian Church in upstate New York and stayed for a quarter century — from 1873 until 1898. Twice elected Moderator of the Presbytery of Rochester, he edited the Presbyterian Church Herald and sat on the Livingston County Historical Society alongside his pastoral work.

narrative_blocks.header: Compiled the Andrew Ward Family Genealogy
narrative_blocks.body: As Secretary of the Association of Descendants of Andrew Ward, George compiled the family genealogy that traces the Ward line back to East Haddam Connecticut. He also published two Princeton class histories — 'After Nine Years' (1878) and 'The Vigintennial Volume of 1869' (1889) — the latter a finely illustrated twenty-year reunion record.

narrative_blocks.header: Father was former Mayor of Rochester New York
narrative_blocks.body: George was the son of Hon. Levi A. Ward, a former Mayor of Rochester, and the grandson of Dr. Levi Ward, a pioneer of Western New York who had emigrated from East Haddam Connecticut early in the 19th century. He was also a cousin of Prof. Henry A.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin of the Rochester natural-science magnate
narrative_blocks.body: George was a first cousin of Prof. Henry Augustus Ward, the naturalist who founded Ward's Natural Science Establishment in Rochester. The firm supplied geological, zoological, and anthropological specimens to universities and museums across the United States — and the cousins shared the Rochester Ward extended family's scholarly orientation, George turning to ministry and genealogy where Henry turned to natural history.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandfather pioneered Western New York from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: George's grandfather Dr. Levi Ward emigrated from East Haddam Connecticut early in the 19th century and was among the pioneer settlers of Western New York, establishing the Ward family in Rochester. The migration west from a Connecticut town that itself had been a 17th-century Puritan settlement carried the family's New England Calvinist culture into the Genesee Valley.

cross_connections.display_label: Granddaughter Carolyn Pierpont Ward carries her name forward two ways

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter's marriage produced a Hooker cousin convergence
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline married Rev. George Kemp Ward in 1873 and bore four children — Levi, Ruth, Charles, and Kenneth — all Hooker descendants through her Pierpont line. When her daughter Ruth married Horace Willard Hooker in 1906, the union produced the project's third documented Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage, with both spouses tracing descent to Rev. Thomas Hooker through different branches.

burialCemetery.name: Mount Hope Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: One of America's oldest municipal Victorian cemeteries (founded 1838 — pre-dating the famous Mt. Auburn). The Gordon family plot is in Lot 61 Range 2 — purchased by Luther Gordon Sr. (Brockport bank founder, 1818-1881). His son George Cooley Gordon Sr. (X02281, NOTABLE) paid $9,229.54 for perpetual care of the plot in 1883. The plot features a stunning monument with columns and an allegorical female figure with drapery, one hand across her heart and the other holding a cross, gazing into the distance. Modest stones below in a square perimeter — except for infant William 'Willie' Gordon's stone (HD3268, 1879-1880) prominently set below the central angel monument. Hooker-line burials at Mt. Hope: George Cooley Gordon Sr (X02281), Ida May Hooker Gordon (H03892, with monument), William 'Willie' Gordon (HD3268), George Cooley Gordon II (HD3262), Cecelia Mitchell Kimball Gordon (X02282), George Cooley Gordon III (HD3263), Cornelia Macy Hawks Gordon (X02283), Cecilia 'Trottie' Gordon (HD3264), Laura Kimball Gordon (HD3265), Fred Hooker Gordon Sr (HD3266) with separate Section MM Lot 266 plot, Florence Robinson Gordon (X02284), Fred Hooker Gordon Jr (HD-prefix), Virginia DeVilbiss Gordon (X-prefix), Fred Hooker Gordon III (HD-prefix, infant Boston meningioma), Lucius Robinson 'Bob' Gordon (HD-prefix, NOTABLE), Marie Antoinette 'Tony' Townson Gordon (X-prefix), William Ward Gordon (HD-prefix), Emilie Wolcott Gordon (X-prefix), William Ward Gordon Jr (HD-prefix back in original Lot 61 Range 2). Largest Hooker-tree concentration after Cypress Lawn SF.
